slides.title: Code That Makes Decisions
slides.body: Every useful program needs to choose: show an error or a success? Let the user in or not? **`if` statements** are how Python picks a path — they are the heartbeat of all program logic.
slides.tip: if / elif / else is used in almost every real program ever written.

slides.title: Your First if Statement
slides.body: Write `if condition:` with a **colon**, then indent the code you want to run. Python only enters that block when the condition is `True`.
slides.tip: The 4-space indent is not optional — it tells Python which lines belong inside the if.

slides.title: else: the Fallback Branch
slides.body: Add `else:` to run a **different** block when the condition is `False`. Exactly one of the two branches always runs.
slides.tip: else never has a condition — it's the catch-all at the end.

slides.title: elif: Check More Options
slides.body: Need more than two paths? Use **`elif`** ("else if") to test extra conditions. Python checks them **top to bottom** and stops at the first `True` one.
slides.tip: Order matters — put the most specific (highest) conditions first.

slides.title: You've Got if / elif / else!
slides.body: You can now give Python a brain: ask a question with `if`, add a fallback with `else`, and handle extra cases with `elif`. Programs that make decisions are programs that actually *do* things.
slides.tip: Bonus: `label = "Win" if score >= 100 else "Try again"` squeezes a simple if/else into one line.
